section_title: INTRODUCTION TO 20 PIPS PER DAY SCALPING
content:
This lesson is the third in the 2017 ICT Mentorship, focusing on 'ICT Amplified Day Trading and Scalping:
20 Pips Per Day'. The objective is targeting 20 Pips per day. It is important to preface that you will not make
20 Pips every day; no one can promise that. Not every trading day offers the same opportunity; some days
have more potential, others none at all. Traders must be mindful of this.

Ideally, aim to bank 20 Pips in any day trade. The surest way of banking 20 Pips for the day is to take
profits at this level in every trade. While not advocating daily trading, if you do, always aim to bank
something at 20 Pips. There are techniques to scalp 20 Pips 'almost' every day. The trader must do their
homework to determine the current market environment, such as consolidation, reversing, or expanding.
This is entirely about Market Profiling. This lesson will teach two methods to scalp with an aim for 20 Pips.

- OVERVIEW AND BUY SETUP
This method addresses how to trade the Asian session for those who need to, despite the lower volume
compared to London and New York. This technique, or pattern, is a complete system.
- We are trading the 15-minute New York session stops.
- This pattern is good for Yen, Aussie, and Kiwi crosses.
- The buy setup occurs during the Asian session, up to 12:00 a.m. New York time.
- You will scout short-term lows formed in the New York session. This typically refers to very late New York
session, transitioning into the beginning of the New York crossover into Asia (or New Zealand and Aussie
open).
- We trade long after Asia probes the low, essentially looking for a 'Turtle Soup long'.

- SELL SETUP AND TRADE PARAMETERS
The selling setup is during the Asian session, up to 12:00 a.m. New York time.
- We scout short-term highs formed in the New York session.
- We trade short after Asia probes these highs.
- Timing is off a five-minute chart.
- Targeting 20 Pips, which is a fixed target. No attempt to graduate or turn it into a massive profit.
- It's a straight 20 Pips target with a 20 Pips stop.

section_title: SHORT SETUP ABOVE LATE NEW YORK HIGHS
content:
An example shows a short-term high formed prior to the Asian session start. This short-term high will have
a small pocket of stops resting above it, delineated as 'late New York stops'. This is interpreted as a very
low volume liquidity run, essentially defining the Asian Range High. If price trades above that short-term
high (prior to the Asian session start) on a five-minute basis, we look to go short and take 20 Pips. The blue
ranges in the video delineate what 20 Pips looks like, even if price moves further.

- RANGE EXPECTATIONS AND CONSOLIDATION
The setup usually occurs before New York midnight. We are trading inside the Asian range, expecting it to
behave as it typically does: a consolidation. Many times, the Asian high forms after a short-term high is
taken out. Once that high is taken out, they fade it and move price in the opposite direction, forming the
Asian range. While the Asian range can often be larger than 20-40 Pips, it is many times at least enough to
get 20 Pips out of it.

- DOLLAR YEN SHORT EXAMPLE AND PD ARRAY CONTEXT
Another example with the Dollar Yen shows a short-term high where price trades above it during the Asian
session. Fading this move yields 20 Pips before midnight during the defined Asian range. Between 8:00
p.m. New York time, a small rally is seen, which is then faded, allowing price to trade down 20 Pips.
Looking at the far left of the chart, a fair value gap is visible, which price ultimately reached for, indicating
more downside potential beyond the 20 Pips. This makes these Asian trades favorable, not for getting rich
with 100 Pips, but for utilizing PD Matrix concepts (premium and discount) on lower timeframes.

- STOP LOSS AND ENTRY AGGRESSION
Another Dollar Yen example illustrates price trading above a short-term high in the Asian session. We
anticipate consolidation, so there's nothing to fear. We are looking for the general sideways environment
the Asian session creates. If price keeps running, your stop loss is fixed at 20 Pips, just like any other trade.
This serves as a filter to prevent advanced losses. When trading above a short-term high, the entry is to
sell short. The ideal entry is around five Pips above the short-term highs. Aggressive traders might enter
one or two Pips above. If a five-Pip entry isn't available, miss the trade.

- TRADE LOGIC AND HIGHER TIMEFRAME ALIGNMENT
Another example shows a short-term high rallied above in the Asian session, creating the Asian range
high. Price then fades this, yielding 20 Pips. Even if trades appear to continue on the upside or downside,
the focus is on the initial 'whip' above a short-term high or below a short-term low. In market continuations,
especially if bullish, selling above a short-term high works better. This is because you are pricing in the
Asian Range High, and for upside movement, a Judas Swing down is expected. This allows for anticipating
an Asian range that trades lower, potentially making a Judas Swing and delivering 20 Pips before price
reverses higher for the day's close, without necessarily relying on a London session Judas Swing.

- KIWI DOLLAR BUY EXAMPLE
An example with the Kiwi Dollar shows the beginning of the Asian session with a short-term low. Price
trades down below this short-term low, probes it, takes out the liquidity below it, and then rallies up. The
box represents 20 Pips, and price continues further. The objective is simply to find an opportunity to trade
between 8:00 p.m. and midnight New York time.

- INTRODUCTION
The second method for scalping 20 Pips involves trading the New York expansion. This pattern is universal
and good for all currency pairs, without any specific favorite. It's universally applicable.

- BUY SETUP
The buy setup occurs during the New York session, up to 10:00 a.m. New York time.
- We scout short-term lows formed in the New York open.
- Trading long after New York probes these lows.
- A key condition is that the London session has already posted the daily low, and the 5-day average daily
range (ADR) is still pending (has not been filled for the day).
- Price has rallied from the London low, is retracing in New York, and trades down below a 5-minute short
- term low.
- When this occurs, we buy long below that short-term low, with the mindset that it's a 'Turtle Soup' move,
coming back for short-term sell stops, anticipating expansion towards the 5-day ADR.

- SELL SETUP AND PARAMETERS
The sell setup is the opposite scenario, also during the New York session, up to 10:00 a.m. New York time.
- We scout short-term highs formed in the New York open.
- Trading short after New York probes these highs.
- London session must have posted the daily high (or is highly favorable of being the daily high), and the 5
- day average daily range for that day is pending or yet to be fulfilled.
- New York session has a small retracement higher, trading above a 5-minute short-term high, taking out
buy stops above a 5-minute swing high.
- The average daily range for the last 5 days' low has not been met yet. This is the condition for the trade.
- Timing is off a 5-minute chart.
- Targeting 20 Pips, fixed with a stop loss at 20 Pips.

- AUSSIE DOLLAR BUY EXAMPLE
An example with the Aussie Dollar on a five-minute chart shows price making a low in London, followed by
expansion and retracement. On the 5-minute chart, a short-term low is violated, and price trades back
down into a 5-minute fair value gap and an order block. Price then expands well beyond 20 Pips. This
specific pattern was the reason for anticipating a higher high for the Aussie Dollar during a live New York
session.

- ADDITIONAL BUY EXAMPLE AND EURO DOLLAR SELL
EXAMPLE
Another example shows London creating a potential low of the day, followed by upside expansion during
the New York session. Price comes down below a 5-minute short-term low, quickly rejects, and goes up
much more than 20 Pips (20 Pips shaded in blue).
- For the Euro Dollar, the London open creates the high of the day. Price trades down, and a retracement in
the New York session comes back above a 5-minute short-term high for buy stops that have been trailed
down. Once the short-term high is violated, selling short above it targets 20 Pips, which is easily achieved.

- DOLLAR CAD MULTIPLE OPPORTUNITIES AND CONVICTION
Sometimes, multiple setups can occur in the same trading day, even for a generally low-volatility pair like
the Dollar CAD. Trading off a 5-minute chart offers this potential, especially on choppy days with later
afternoon expansion.
- Example 1: A London low forms, price expands, taking out an early Frankfurt high. Price then retraces,
violates a 5-minute short-term low in the New York open, takes stops, and rallies 20 Pips.
- Example 2: A later opportunity occurs where another 5-minute low is violated, and price expands well
beyond 20 Pips again.
The premise for these trades requires strong conviction about what London has already done and looking
for that continuation in the New York session.

- IDEAL SCENARIO AND UNIVERSAL APPLICATION
The ideal scenario is to look for a 5-minute low and allow price to break down below it. This entices short
sellers and breakout artists, while also knocking out existing long positions that would profit from the next
leg up. This pattern is a strict guideline for Forex, and also very effective for S&P (ES) trading, Dow futures,
Triple Q's, and stock day trading on a 5-minute chart. It's a universal pattern applicable to commodities,
bonds, and other instruments.
CONCLUSION
- FOCUSED APPROACH AND PRACTICE
These two concepts provide a more focused approach for finding short-term intraday opportunities. While
not always present, by reviewing a large number of pairs (10-15), such setups can be found almost every
trading day for practice. The emphasis is on 'practice' to avoid thinking one can day trade every single day
for 20 Pips, but rather to identify such panning-out opportunities daily across various pairs.
